title: The Night That You Told Me Those Not-So-Little White Lies
summary: "According to ex-President Obama's political director, David Simas, religious institutions, academia, and the media have traditionally set the limits of acceptable discourse. Now, through Facebook and Twitter, one can get around this restriction. This creates a whole new permission structure, a sense of social affirmation for what was once thought unthinkable."
body: So we have a lying President. They keep rolling out of his orotund-shaped mouth viz. The attendance at his inauguration in 2017 exceeded that of Obama eight years earlier. He would have won the popular vote as well as the electoral vote in 2016 had the opposition not infiltrated thousands of illegal aliens onto the voter rolls. Obama "wiretapped" him. But does it really matter that our President has a penchant for non-truth? The New York Times maintains that it does, and it recently devoted a one-page spread containing the message that truth is all-important. According to ex-President Obama's political director, David Simas, religious institutions, academia, and the media have traditionally set the limits of acceptable discourse. Now, through Facebook and Twitter, one can get around this restriction. This creates a whole new permission structure, a sense of social affirmation for what was once thought unthinkable. Or as Barack Obama himself put it: The new media ecosystem "means everything is true and nothing is true." These observations are contained in an interview that David Remnick had with Barack Obama and some of his colleagues, published in the November 28, 2016 issue of The New Yorker . But what are foreign leaders to think of this prevaricating President who, steeped in a show-biz mentality for much of his adult life, has little regard for or consciousness of the larger truths behind America’s foreign policy. What if he acts on his imperfect knowledge? _list.source: Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs, Harvard Kennedy School

result.main_content_en: The article critiques a U.S. President's propensity for significant falsehoods, citing specific claims about inauguration attendance, voter fraud, and alleged wiretapping. It questions the impact of this disregard for truth, referencing The New York Times's emphasis on truth's importance. The piece explores how traditional institutions like media and academia once bounded acceptable discourse, but new social media platforms create a 'permission structure' that socially affirms previously unthinkable statements, leading to an ecosystem where 'everything is true and nothing is true,' as noted by Barack Obama. The central concern shifts to foreign policy, pondering how foreign leaders perceive a President with a show-business mentality who may act on imperfect or false knowledge, potentially undermining America's international stance and credibility.
result.main_content_zh: 本文批评了一位美国总统惯于发表重大不实言论的行为，具体列举了关于就职典礼人数、选民欺诈以及所谓窃听等虚假声明。文章质疑这种漠视真相的后果，并引用了《纽约时报》对真相重要性的强调。文中探讨了媒体、学术界等传统机构曾如何界定可接受的言论边界，而脸书和推特等新媒体平台则创造了一种“许可结构”，为曾经不可想象的言论提供了社会认同，导致了一个“一切皆真又一切皆假”的生态系统，正如巴拉克·奥巴马所指出的。文章的核心关切转向外交政策，思考外国领导人如何看待一位具有演艺圈思维模式、可能依据有缺陷或错误的知识行事的总统，这可能会损害美国的国际立场和信誉。
